scenario: Spring Boot app needs to deploy a BPMN file after specific custom setup (e.g., DB seed). Team wonders alternative to @Deployment annotation that allows ordering. @PostConstruct deployment for custom timing?
question: @PostConstruct deployment for custom timing?

options.text: @Deployment is indeed one approach for specifying which BPMN resources to deploy with a Camunda application. This is useful for conditional deployment logic that @Deployment cannot express.

Programmatic deployment via @PostConstruct or @EventListener for custom timing: @PostConstruct: method runs after Spring bean initialised. class): after full app start (all beans + auto-config ready). Order: control via Spring's bean ordering (@DependsOn, constructor DI, or @Order annotation). Use cases: deploy after DB migrations; after custom Connector registration; conditional deploys per env. newDeployResourceCommand()… for programmatic deployment.

Deploying new process definitions in a Camunda Spring Boot Starter application always requires a full application restart -- this approach has limitations. While the @Deployment annotation and @PostConstruct/@EventListener programmatic deployment run at startup, the CamundaClient can also be injected into any Spring component and used to call newDeployResourceCommand() at any time during application runtime -- for example, triggered by an admin REST endpoint, a configuration change event, or a scheduled task. This enables hot-deployment of updated process definitions without restarting the Spring Boot application.

The Camunda Spring Boot Starter provides no programmatic API for deploying process resources -- only the declarative @Deployment annotation is available -- this approach has limitations. yaml) exposes the full Zeebe gRPC and REST API surface including newDeployResourceCommand(). This command accepts classpath resources, file system files, or in-memory byte arrays and deploys them to the connected cluster. 8).

a.text: Incorrect. Programmatic deployment timing: Pattern 1: @PostConstruct: `java @Component public class ProcessDeployer { @Autowired private CamundaClient client; // C8.8: CamundaClient, not ZeebeClient @Autowired private DatabaseMigrationService dbMigration; @PostConstruct public void deployProcesses() { // Ensure DB migration ran first via dependency dbMigration.ensureMigrated(); // Then deploy client.newDeployResourceCommand() .addResourceFromClasspath("processes/order.bpmn") .send() .join(); } } Pros: Custom timing: after specific setup. Programmatic control: error handling, conditional deploys. Cons: Boilerplate: more code than @Deployment annotation. Bean lifecycle dependencies: ensure correct ordering. Pattern 2: @EventListener(ApplicationReadyEvent.class): java @Component public class ProcessDeployer { @Autowired private CamundaClient client; @EventListener(ApplicationReadyEvent.class) public void deployOnReady() { // App fully started; deploy client.newDeployResourceCommand() .addResourceFromClasspath("processes/order.bpmn") .send() .join(); } } Pros: After full app start: all beans initialised. Decoupled: not tied to specific bean's @PostConstruct. Pattern 3: Ordered bean initialisation: java @Component @DependsOn("databaseMigrator") // deploy after migrator public class ProcessDeployer { // @PostConstruct deployment } Spring ensures databaseMigrator initialised first. Pattern 4: Conditional deployment: java @PostConstruct public void deploy() { if (shouldDeploy()) { // deploy logic } } private boolean shouldDeploy() { // check env, feature flag, etc. return env.isDeployEnabled(); } Skip deploy in some environments (e.g., production via CI/CD). Pattern 5: Programmatic with batching: java @EventListener(ApplicationReadyEvent.class) public void deployAll() { Resource[] processes = ... // find all BPMN files DeployResourceCommandStep1 command = zeebeClient.newDeployResourceCommand(); for (Resource r : processes) { command = command.addResourceFromClasspath(r.getPath()); } command.send().join(); } Single deployment with multiple resources (atomic). For the scenario (deploy after DB setup): java @Component public class CustomDeployer { @Autowired private CamundaClient client; // 8.8 unified client (replaces ZeebeClient) @Autowired private DbSetupService dbSetup; @EventListener(ApplicationReadyEvent.class) public void deployAfterSetup() { dbSetup.runMigrations(); dbSetup.seedData(); client.newDeployResourceCommand() .addResourceFromClasspath("processes/order.bpmn") .send() .join(); } } Comparison with @Deployment`: | Aspect | @Deployment | Programmatic | |--------|-------------|--------------| | Boilerplate | Minimal | More code | | Timing control | Auto on startup | Custom | | Error handling | Implicit | Explicit | | Conditional | Limited | Full | | Custom setup deps | Hard | Easy | Best practices: Use @Deployment for simple cases. Use programmatic for custom timing / conditions. Log deployments: track which resources deployed. Error handling: explicit; don't silently fail. Testing considerations: Test deploys in dev / staging. Mock ZeebeClient in unit tests. For production: Typically CI/CD-driven (per Set 14 Q53 / Q56). App auto-deploy useful for dev / staging. Don't double-deploy in prod.
